name: Morgan Banking Power
description: The concept of 'Morgan Banking Power' refers to a conspiracy theory alleging that the J.P. Morgan banking dynasty, and its associated financial institutions, wielded (and perhaps continue to wield) undue and often secretive influence over American and global economic and political affairs. Proponents claim this power was used to manipulate markets, instigate wars for profit, and control government policy. The theory originates from criticisms of the Morgan family's extensive financial dealings in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, particularly their role in stabilizing the economy during financial panics and their involvement in funding World War I. Evidence cited includes historical accounts of Morgan's financial leverage, perceived connections between Morgan-affiliated individuals and political figures, and analyses of economic trends attributed to deliberate manipulation. Mainstream historians and economists generally acknowledge the significant influence of the Morgan family but reject the notion of a clandestine, malevolent agenda. They argue that Morgan's actions were largely motivated by profit and a desire to maintain financial stability, albeit with significant consequences. Critics point to a lack of concrete evidence supporting claims of deliberate manipulation and highlight the complexity of economic and political systems, arguing that attributing events solely to the actions of a single family is overly simplistic and ignores other contributing factors. The theory often overlaps with broader anti-Semitic tropes, as some interpretations associate Morgan power with Jewish bankers and international finance.
subtitle: Alleged Financial Control and Manipulation

claims.claim: J.P. Morgan instigated or profited from wars.
claims.evidence: Morgan's financing of WWI and subsequent profits are cited as evidence of profiting from war.
claims.criticism: While Morgan financed Allied war efforts, this was a legitimate business transaction, and attributing war causation solely to financial motives is simplistic. Morgan also helped stabilize the US economy during the war.

claims.claim: The Morgan family controls the Federal Reserve.
claims.evidence: The alleged influence of Morgan-affiliated banks in the early development of the Federal Reserve system is presented as proof of control.
claims.criticism: The Federal Reserve is a public entity with oversight from Congress. While private banks initially played a role, the system is designed to be independent of any single family or institution. Regulations and oversight mechanisms are in place to prevent undue influence.

claims.claim: Morgan's influence is used to manipulate stock markets and create economic crises.
claims.evidence: Historical instances of market fluctuations after Morgan family actions or statements are interpreted as deliberate manipulation.
claims.criticism: Attributing market fluctuations solely to Morgan actions ignores the complexity of market dynamics and other contributing factors, such as global events, investor sentiment, and government policies. Correlation does not equal causation.

keyFigures.name: G. Edward Griffin
keyFigures.role: Promoter of conspiracy theories related to the Federal Reserve and banking power.

keyFigures.name: Murray Rothbard
keyFigures.role: Libertarian economist critical of central banking and its perceived power.

keyFigures.name: Ron Chernow
keyFigures.role: Biographer of J.P. Morgan, providing a balanced historical account.

mythologyConnections: Leviathan (as a symbol of powerful financial entities)
Mammon (the personification of greed and wealth)

timeline.event: J.P. Morgan consolidates his banking empire.
J.P. Morgan helps avert a financial panic, solidifying his reputation and influence.
The Federal Reserve Act is passed, leading to claims of Morgan influence in its creation.
J.P. Morgan finances Allied war efforts, generating significant profits and fueling conspiracy theories.

sources.author: Ron Chernow
sources.work: The House of Morgan: An American Banking Dynasty and the Rise of Modern Finance
sources.title: Ron Chernow
